key_insight: SIGMA shows that multi-agent performance improves not only by optimizing communication, but also by composing agents from reusable skills via a task-conditioned skill-incidence graph.
engineering_relevance: For computational engineering workflows, this suggests a more flexible way to build task-specific agent teams that can generalize to new capability combinations without redesigning the whole system.
method: Predict a skill-agent incidence matrix from the task and skill library.
Construct each agent embedding by composing selected skills.
Decode inter-agent communication topology and route messages through skill-specific mailboxes.
evidence_signal: Across six reasoning and coding benchmarks with three base LLMs, SIGMA achieved the best average performance and outperformed CARD by 2.06, 2.36, and 1.75 points on the three base models, with only a 0.96-point average drop under unseen skill libraries.
watch_item: The approach depends on the quality and coverage of the predefined skill library, so missing or poorly defined skills could limit compositional gains.
paper_title: SIGMA: Skill-Incidence Graphs for Compositional Multi-Agent Design
